title: Introducing Cursor 3
summary: Cursor released version 3 of its AI-powered code editor, positioning it as simpler and more powerful while designed for a future where code is written by agents. The tool maintains development environment depth alongside agent integration.
writeup: Cursor 3 is announced as a unified home for coding agents, merging a professional IDE (language servers, files, remote SSH) with the ability to run multiple agents simultaneously on local machines or in the cloud. The central workflow shift: developers talk to agents, review their changes, and take them through to a merged PR, with local and cloud execution seamlessly interchangeable. Cursor positions this release as the convergence of three pieces built over prior weeks: the Cloud runtime (sandboxed end-to-end code execution), Composer 2 (a frontier model with high limits and fast speeds), and now the Cursor 3 product shell that ties them together.

key_points: Cursor 3 combines a full professional development environment with multi-agent orchestration, allowing any number of agents to run concurrently, either locally or in cloud sandboxes, from a single interface.

The new workflow centers on agent delegation: a developer can pull an agent locally for close iteration, then hand it back to the cloud to run autonomously, then return to inspect diffs and approve a PR without leaving Cursor.
Cursor Cloud, announced a month prior, gives agents their own sandboxed runtime capable of running code end-to-end on full projects, forming the execution layer beneath Cursor 3.

Composer 2, announced weeks before Cursor 3, is described as a frontier model with very high token limits and fast inference speeds, serving as the model layer in the three-part stack of runtime, model, and product.
The stated goal is for agents to feel like colleagues: ambient, parallel, and capable of taking a project from prompt to merged PR without the developer managing each step.
